name: r.jina.ai hosted service
applicability: Directly usable as a fetch backend for /website-analysis and Content Analysis System (T28). Prepend URL → get LLM-friendly markdown. Free tier + paid tiers. Handles SPA, PDFs, JS-rendered pages, cookies, proxies out of the box. No setup required.
home_connection: CAS pipeline currently uses own fetch logic (superpowers-chrome for /website-analysis). r.jina.ai could be a fallback path when Chrome isn't practical, OR the primary for sources where TLS fingerprinting matters. JASON-OS could expose this as a portable content fetch primitive.

name: s.jina.ai search-grounding service
applicability: Search query → top-5 URLs → fetched + extracted in one call. Alternative to manually chaining a SERP API + a fetcher. Useful if CAS ever adds a 'search and analyze' flow.
home_connection: Not in active roadmap, but could slot into deep-research's web search phase. Current deep-research uses WebSearch + WebFetch; s.jina.ai could replace both in a single call.

name: x-* request header protocol
applicability: 11+ `x-*` headers control runtime fetch behavior (target-selector, wait-for-selector, timeout, cache tolerance, no-cache, respond-with, proxy-url, set-cookie, with-generated-alt, engine, return-format). Clean separation of URL identity from fetch configuration. Pattern is portable — SoNash's CAS skills could expose a similar header vocabulary for /website-analysis custom modes.
home_connection: CAS skills take config via slash-command flags (--depth=quick). For programmatic invocation or future MCP exposure, a header-based config surface is worth studying.

name: Readability + Turndown + Puppeteer extraction stack
applicability: Reference implementation of the standard HTML→markdown extraction pipeline: Puppeteer (render) → linkedom (DOM parse) → @mozilla/readability (content narrow) → Turndown + GFM plugin (markdown). Runs at commercial scale. Every stage swappable via DI.
home_connection: If /website-analysis ever needs richer extraction than superpowers-chrome provides (edge cases like SPAs with hash routing, preloaded shells), this is the canonical stack to port. Per Skip dep: @mozilla/readability is the industry standard.

name: curl-impersonate as Docker base layer (LD_PRELOAD TLS spoofing)
applicability: LD_PRELOAD a Chrome-fingerprinted libcurl so every outbound hop impersonates Chrome 116's TLS stack. Defeats Cloudflare-style fingerprint blocks that firecrawl/crawl4ai leak through. Docker two-stage: `FROM lwthiker/curl-impersonate:0.6-chrome-slim-bullseye` → `FROM node:22`, then copy `.so` to second stage.
home_connection: Not immediately relevant to SoNash's current Firebase deployment (no self-hosted fetcher). But if Jason ever containerizes CAS as a standalone fetcher (JASON-OS primitive), this is the move to defeat anti-bot.

name: Multi-target deployment via --args override
applicability: One Docker image deployed 6 times to Cloud Run by varying --args build/stand-alone/<entry>.js. Inside container, tsyringe DI resolves the right API host class. Zero code duplication across runtimes; divergence is at DI boundary.
home_connection: SoNash is Firebase-only (Cloud Functions). This pattern is Cloud Run-specific. Not directly applicable, but the idea of 'one image, many roles via args' is reusable if SoNash ever leaves Functions.

name: Multi-provider fallback generator (iterProviders)
applicability: Generator that yields provider clients in preference order (Jina internal → Serper Google → Serper Bing). A for...of loop catches per-provider errors and advances. First success wins. Query-shortening fallback strips last N/4 terms, up to 4 iterations, RTL-aware.
home_connection: Direct fit for deep-research's cross-model verification phase, which already tries multiple models. Same generator pattern would work for multi-SERP, multi-transcript-source (media-analysis), multi-embedding-provider, etc. Port as a generic `iterProvidersWithFallback` utility.

name: Adaptive crawler as Cloud Tasks queue
applicability: Queue-based async crawl that breaks large-site crawling into Cloud Tasks. Each task persists state to Firestore, polls, and progresses. Decouples client request from crawl completion.
home_connection: Not needed — SoNash's analysis skills are interactive and bounded (one repo, one page). If CAS ever adds bulk 'analyze 100 URLs from this list', this pattern applies; until then, filed as reference.

name: Full-site fetching Colab notebook
applicability: Only practical example of multi-URL crawling with r.jina.ai. Methodology for full-site extraction — URL enumeration, rate limiting, result merging.
home_connection: Worth reading IF user wants to add a 'bulk analyze' mode to website-analysis. Not urgent. Routes to Coverage Audit if user wants deeper fetch.

name: Blog: Reader for search-grounding to improve factuality of LLMs
applicability: Conceptual rationale for s.jina.ai: LLMs hallucinate less when grounded on actual fetched web content. The search-then-read pattern (vs search-only) gives grounding. Numbers and examples in the post.
home_connection: Relevant to deep-research's evidence-gathering philosophy. Same thesis: search alone gives snippets; fetch + extract gives truth. Jason's CAS already subscribes to this — the post is validation not novelty.

name: DeepWiki auto-generated wiki
applicability: Third-party auto-generated architecture wiki. Substitute for missing first-party docs. Useful if I (or a deep-plan) need to understand internal flow without reading every file.
home_connection: Not directly usable for SoNash. More interesting as evidence that repos CAN substitute real docs with DeepWiki — anti-pattern to note, not to emulate.

name: @mozilla/readability (npm)
applicability: The content narrowing library behind r.jina.ai and Firefox Reader Mode. Extracts 'article body' from arbitrary HTML. Works offline on cached HTML. No render step required if HTML is already static.
home_connection: If /website-analysis ever wants a lightweight fetch path (no Puppeteer), HTML + @mozilla/readability covers the happy path for static sites. Small dep footprint.

name: DeepWiki badge as 'How it works' section
applicability: README's 'How it works' is literally an image badge linking to DeepWiki. Zero first-party architecture exposition in a 10.5k-star OSS repo. Signals: the team doesn't consider understandability a priority for contributors.
home_connection: Directly anti-pattern for SoNash CAS docs. Own your architecture explanation; don't outsource to auto-wiki. File as anti-pattern candidate.

name: Private submodule required for self-host
applicability: Public repo imports from private `thinapps-shared` (rate-limit, secrets, DAOs, decorators, 3rd-party clients). README calls it 'not integral to the Reader's functions' — but the public repo doesn't compile without it. 'Open source' without self-host = showroom, not kit.
home_connection: Anti-pattern for anyone shipping 'open source' infra. If you want to ship as OSS, make it runnable from source. If you want closed, ship closed. The middle ground erodes trust. File as anti-pattern.

name: Zero automated tests in a 10k-star production service
applicability: No .test.ts, no .spec.ts, no describe(), no it(). `firebase-functions-test` is a devDep but never imported. CI is deploy-only (tag push → 6 Cloud Run services). `dry-run` bootstraps DI but doesn't test behavior. Quality floor is TypeScript strict + lint + live traffic.
home_connection: Relevant to SoNash's test philosophy. Reader is evidence that production at scale is possible without tests IF you have observability, traffic volume, and short deploy cycles. But it's a high-stakes bet — any fork without that monitoring is flying blind. File as cautionary anti-pattern: 'no tests' works for originators, not adopters.
